canonical_summary:
10 CFR Part 53 is the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission's risk-informed, technology-inclusive licensing framework for commercial nuclear plants, codified as an optional alternative to 10 CFR Parts 50 and 52. The final rule was published at 91 FR 15694 on March 30, 2026, with an effective date of April 29, 2026, under RIN 3150-AK31 and Docket NRC-2019-0062. It discharges Section 103(a)(4) of the Nuclear Energy Innovation and Modernization Act of 2018 (Pub. L. 115-439) roughly 21 months before the December 31, 2027 statutory deadline.

The rule is organized into eight substantive subparts. Subpart A sets general provisions and definitions, including licensing-basis events, design-basis accidents, and the safety-related versus non-safety-related but safety-significant classification. Subpart B fixes the safety objectives, including the 25 rem TEDE dose criterion at the exclusion area boundary and low population zone under Section 53.210, the frequency-consequence criteria for non-DBA LBEs under Section 53.220, the safety function and functional design criteria of Section 53.230, LBE selection in Section 53.240, and defense in depth in Section 53.250. Subpart C carries the design and analysis requirements, including the PRA and systematic risk evaluation regime of Section 53.450. Subpart D establishes siting, including the Section 53.530 alternative siting provision for higher population density areas.

Subparts E and F govern construction, manufacturing, and operation, including the in-factory fuel loading authority in Section 53.620(d), the generally licensed reactor operator regime for self-reliant-mitigation facilities in Sections 53.805 through 53.820, and the human factors expectations applicable to remote operations centers. Subpart G governs decommissioning financial assurance, the PSDAR, and license termination. Subpart H consolidates licenses, certifications, and approvals, including the PSAR content in Section 53.1309, the OL FSAR in Section 53.1369, and the COL FSAR in Section 53.1416. Together these subparts replace the deterministic, light-water-anchored architecture of Parts 50 and 52 with a performance-based regime built around quantitative health objectives and graded special treatments.
h1: 10 CFR Part 53 subparts: a deep dive for advanced reactor builders
meta_title: 10 CFR Part 53 subparts: a builder's deep dive
meta_description: Section-by-section reference to 10 CFR Part 53 subparts A through H, including the 25 rem TEDE criterion, PRA scope, siting, FSAR, and rule history.

sections.heading: Subpart A: general provisions, scope, and definitions
paragraphs: Subpart A is the vocabulary layer for Part 53. It states that the regulation is a risk-informed, performance-based, technology-inclusive alternative to Parts 50 and 52 that applicants and licensees may voluntarily elect. The codified text is browsable on the eCFR at the Part 53 landing page.

The defined terms drive every downstream demonstration. A Licensing-Basis Event (LBE) is the full set of event sequences considered in design and licensing, partitioned by expected frequency into anticipated, unlikely, and very unlikely sequences. A Design-Basis Accident (DBA) is a deterministically postulated sequence used to set functional design criteria for safety-related structures, systems, and components. Part 53 keeps a two-tier classification: Safety-Related SSCs and Non-Safety-Related but Safety-Significant (NSRST) SSCs, rather than the binary split inherited from Part 50.

Subpart A also defines defense in depth as the inclusion of two or more independent and redundant layers such that no single layer is exclusively relied upon, sets standard provisions on written communications, deliberate misconduct, and employee protection, and confirms that satisfying Part 53 substitutes for the corresponding provisions of Parts 50, 52, 73, and others.
bullets: Voluntary alternative to Parts 50 and 52, with cross-walks to Part 73 security and Part 26 fitness for duty
Two-tier SSC classification: Safety-Related and Non-Safety-Related but Safety-Significant
LBE frequency partition: anticipated, unlikely, and very unlikely event sequences
Defense in depth defined as independent and redundant layers, not single-failure mechanics

sections.heading: Subpart B: technology-inclusive safety requirements (sections 53.200 series)
paragraphs: Subpart B is the safety core. It establishes the quantitative criteria every Part 53 plant must meet regardless of technology, then leaves design and analysis methods to the applicant. The operative sections sit in the 53.200 series, not the 53.100 series that appeared in early proposed-rule drafts.

Section 53.210 codifies the dose-based design-basis accident criterion. For each DBA, the applicant must show that an individual at any point on the exclusion area boundary for any 2-hour period after release onset, and an individual at the outer boundary of the low population zone for the entire passage of the radioactive cloud, would not receive a total effective dose equivalent in excess of 25 rem. The 25 rem value is a regulatory yardstick rather than an acceptable emergency dose limit, consistent with the longstanding position in 10 CFR 50.34 and 50.67.

Section 53.220 supplies the risk-informed criteria for LBEs other than DBAs through frequency-consequence targets. Section 53.230 requires identification of safety functions and the assignment of design features, human actions, and programmatic controls to fulfill them. Section 53.240 sets the LBE selection process. Section 53.250 is the defense-in-depth provision, requiring measures that compensate for uncertainties in modeling, barrier performance, SSC and personnel reliability, and programmatic controls. Section 53.260 carries forward the position from 10 CFR 50.13 that licensees are not required to design specifically against enemy attack.
bullets: Section 53.210: 25 rem TEDE at EAB (2-hour) and LPZ (cloud passage) for each DBA
Section 53.220: frequency-consequence criteria for anticipated, unlikely, and very unlikely LBEs
Section 53.230: safety function identification and functional design criteria
Section 53.240: systematic LBE selection across frequency categories
Section 53.250: defense in depth as compensation for analytical uncertainty

sections.heading: Subpart C: design and analysis requirements (sections 53.400 to 53.490)
paragraphs: Subpart C is the analytical engine. It contains design features for LBEs at Section 53.400, functional design criteria for DBAs and non-DBA LBEs at Sections 53.410 and 53.420, external hazard protection at Section 53.415, normal-operations and worker-protection requirements at Sections 53.425 and 53.430, design requirements at Section 53.440, analysis requirements at Section 53.450, safety categorization and special treatments at Section 53.460, the reserved Section 53.470, and earthquake engineering at Section 53.480.

Section 53.450(a) requires a PRA, other systematic risk evaluations (SREs), or a combination. Permitting SREs in lieu of a full Capability Category PRA is a deliberate technology-inclusive accommodation for designs such as low-source-term microreactors. Section 53.450(b) requires the analyses to inform LBE selection under Section 53.240, SSC classification under Section 53.460, defense-in-depth adequacy under Section 53.250, plant operating state assessments, internal and external event evaluation, and availability controls and special treatments for SR and NSRST SSCs.

Section 53.450(c) requires PRA and SRE maintenance at least every five years until permanent cessation under Section 53.1070 and upgrade per consensus standards endorsed by the NRC, hooking the rule to ASME/ANS RA-S, Regulatory Guide 1.247 for non-LWR PRA, and Regulatory Guide 1.200. Section 53.450(e) governs analysis of LBEs other than DBAs and Section 53.450(f) requires DBAs to be analyzed using deterministic methods, assuming only SR SSCs identified under Section 53.460 and human actions credited under Subpart F.
bullets: Section 53.450(a): PRA, SREs, or a combination as the analytical foundation
Section 53.450(c): PRA maintained at least every 5 years and upgraded per endorsed standards
Section 53.450(f): deterministic DBA analysis using SR SSCs only, conservative demonstration against Section 53.210
Industry vocabulary (AOOs, DBEs, BDBEs, DBAs) comes from NEI 18-04 and RG 1.233; Part 53 only distinguishes DBAs from other LBEs

sections.heading: Subpart D: siting requirements (sections 53.500 to 53.580)
paragraphs: Subpart D establishes site characterization and population-related requirements. The applicant must address geology, seismology, hydrology, meteorology, demographics, and emergency planning logistics, and must demonstrate that site conditions do not undermine the Subpart B safety criteria.

Section 53.530 is the distinctive innovation: applicants may site plants in higher population density areas, including population centers exceeding roughly 25,000 residents, if they demonstrate that societal risk is acceptable in light of societal benefits. This is a deliberate departure from the low-population-zone preference embedded in 10 CFR Part 100 for Part 50 and Part 52 plants.

Seismic treatment moves from the deterministic Safe Shutdown Earthquake and Operating Basis Earthquake pairing of 10 CFR Part 100 Appendix A and Appendix S to Part 50 to a probabilistic Ground Motion Response Spectrum (GMRS) per Regulatory Guide 1.208, combined with graded ASCE/SEI 43-05 and ANSI/ANS-2.26 Seismic Design Categories (SDC) tied to annual exceedance frequencies. The 0.1 g floor and the SSE/OBE pairing do not propagate into Part 53; the design ground motion floats with the SDC needed to meet Subpart B.
bullets: Section 53.530 alternative siting provision for higher population density sites
GMRS per RG 1.208 replaces single deterministic SSE
Graded SDCs from ASCE/SEI 43-05 and ANSI/ANS-2.26 set seismic performance targets
Detailed implementation guidance pending via revised Regulatory Guide 4.7 and Standard Review Plan updates

sections.heading: Subpart E: construction and manufacturing (sections 53.600 to 53.680)
paragraphs: Subpart E governs activities between construction permit or combined license issuance and fuel load, and also covers reactor fabrication under a separate manufacturing license. The provisions broadly mirror Part 50 construction expectations in a technology-neutral form, requiring programs and procedures to assure that the as-built plant or as-fabricated reactor meets the Subpart B safety criteria.

The signature provision is Section 53.620 on manufacturing. Subsection (d) authorizes in-factory loading of unirradiated fuel into a manufactured reactor before shipment, provided design features prevent the reactor from achieving criticality during transport and remain in place until NRC authorization is given at the installation site. A separate Part 53 license is still required to install and operate the manufactured reactor at its destination.

Construction-era human factors and security controls flow from Subpart C design requirements and from cross-cutting programs that licensees establish under Subparts F, H, and J, including the Subpart H integrity assessment program and quality assurance. Industrial security during construction continues to be governed by Part 73.
bullets: Section 53.620(d) authorizes in-factory loading of unirradiated fuel before shipment
Manufacturing license (ML) is a stand-alone vehicle; site installation requires a separate Part 53 license
Construction-phase QA tied to Appendix B to Part 50 via design references
Security during construction continues under Part 73

sections.heading: Subpart F: requirements for operation (sections 53.700 to 53.890)
paragraphs: Subpart F is the operational core, covering technical specifications, maintenance and surveillance, configuration controls, staffing, training, qualifications, and human factors. The approach is risk-informed, technology-inclusive, and performance-based, with operator requirements scaled to the level of human action credited in the safety analysis.

Section 53.730 allows customized licensed operator staffing tied to the credited human actions. The rule then introduces a two-track operator licensing structure. For self-reliant-mitigation facilities, where the Commission determines the design does not require human action to prevent or mitigate accidents, Sections 53.805 through 53.820 establish generally licensed reactor operators (GLROs) in lieu of individually licensed operators examined under Part 55. For interaction-dependent mitigation facilities, traditional licensed reactor operators and senior reactor operators continue to be required and examined under Part 55.

Subpart F human factors engineering reaches any location where activities support plant safety or emergency response functions, including remote operations centers, removing the strict onsite limitation found in legacy rules. Fitness-for-duty obligations are implemented through new Subpart M of 10 CFR Part 26, which accommodates remote and small-staff facilities. Security obligations are anchored in companion revisions to 10 CFR Part 73 issued alongside Part 53.
bullets: Section 53.730 customized staffing tied to credited human actions
Sections 53.805 to 53.820 generally licensed reactor operators for self-reliant-mitigation facilities
Human factors program extends to remote operations centers
Fitness for duty via Part 26 Subpart M; security via revised Part 73

sections.heading: Subpart G: decommissioning requirements (sections 53.1000 to 53.1080)
paragraphs: Subpart G defines decommissioning obligations attached to operating licenses and combined licenses. Section 53.1000 sets scope and purpose. Sections 53.1010 through 53.1060 address financial assurance, cost estimates, escalation factors, permissible funding mechanisms, oversight, and reporting. Sections 53.1070 through 53.1080 govern the transition from operations through site release and license termination.

Section 53.1010 requires the applicant to certify financial assurance in the amount approved by NRC under Section 53.1020, based on a site-specific cost estimate, adjusted annually under Section 53.1030 using a site-specific factor or a generic factor of at least 0.65L + 0.13E + 0.22B with reference to Bureau of Labor Statistics regional data and NUREG-1307 waste burial charges. Section 53.1040 lists permissible methods: prepayment, external sinking fund, surety or insurance or guarantee, statement of intent for Federal licensees, contractual funding via non-bypassable customer obligations, and other mechanisms acceptable to NRC.

Section 53.1045 limits trust fund use to 3 percent for planning plus an additional 20 percent after the PSDAR. Sections 53.1070 through 53.1080 govern certifications of permanent cessation and fuel removal, the post-shutdown decommissioning activities report (PSDAR), the license termination plan, the decommissioning fire protection program, and the dose criteria of 10 CFR 20.1402 for releasing impacted and non-impacted areas.
bullets: Section 53.1020 site-specific decommissioning cost estimate (DCE)
Section 53.1030 annual escalation factor 0.65L + 0.13E + 0.22B
Section 53.1040 six categories of permissible financial assurance methods
Sections 53.1070 to 53.1080 cessation certifications, PSDAR, license termination plan, and 10 CFR 20.1402 release criteria

sections.heading: Subpart H: licenses, certifications, and approvals (sections 53.1100 to 53.1500 series)
paragraphs: Subpart H is the longest piece of Part 53. It establishes filing mechanics (Section 53.1100), general application content (Section 53.1109), incorporation by reference (Section 53.1106), and the full set of permits and licenses: early site permits, construction permits, operating licenses, combined licenses, standard design approvals, standard design certifications, and manufacturing licenses.

Section 53.1309 specifies PSAR content for construction permit applications. The PSAR must include site information equivalent to that required for an early site permit under Section 53.1146(a)(1)(iv) through (x), design information equivalent to a standard design certification per Section 53.1239, the quality assurance program tied to Appendix B to Part 50, preliminary plans for emergencies based on Appendix E to Part 50 (or 10 CFR 50.160 for SMRs and non-LWRs), preliminary physical security under Section 53.540, a Part 26 fitness-for-duty program, and a Safeguards Information protection program under 10 CFR 73.21 and 73.22.

FSAR content lives in two parallel sections in the final rule: Section 53.1369 for operating license applications and Section 53.1416 for combined license applications. Earlier proposed-rule drafts numbered the COL FSAR section in the Section 53.140x range, but the final published numbering is Section 53.1369 and Section 53.1416. Both sections require integrity assessment, role-of-personnel and human factors analyses under Section 53.730, training and proficiency programs under Sections 53.730(g) and 53.830, emergency plans under Section 53.855, maintenance under Section 53.715, QA under Section 53.865, radiation protection under Section 53.850, physical security and safeguards contingency under Section 53.860 and Part 73, cybersecurity under Section 73.54 or Section 73.110, fire protection under Section 53.875, ISI/IST under Section 53.880, fitness for duty under Part 26, and proposed technical specifications under Section 53.710(a).
bullets: Section 53.1309 PSAR content for CP applications, including Section 53.1239 design references
Section 53.1369 OL FSAR and Section 53.1416 COL FSAR (final-rule numbering)
Incorporation by reference of ESPs, SDAs, SDCs, and MLs with envelope confirmation
Subpart H also houses standard design approvals, standard design certifications, and manufacturing licenses

sections.heading: Rule history: NEIMA, SECY trail, and the 2026 final rule
paragraphs: The originating statute is the Nuclear Energy Innovation and Modernization Act of 2018 (Pub. L. 115-439), signed January 14, 2019. Section 103(a)(4) directs the NRC to complete a rulemaking establishing a technology-inclusive regulatory framework for optional use by commercial advanced reactor applicants no later than December 31, 2027, and to increase use of risk-informed, performance-based licensing techniques.

On April 13, 2020, then-Executive Director Margaret Doane transmitted SECY-20-0032, the rulemaking plan, scoped to commercial advanced nuclear reactors and anchored in the Licensing Modernization Project. The Commission approved the plan via SRM-SECY-20-0032 on October 2, 2020 (ML20254A143). On March 1, 2023, then-Executive Director Daniel Dorman transmitted SECY-23-0021, the draft proposed rule, organized around two voluntary frameworks. The Commission acted via SRM-SECY-23-0021 on March 4, 2024 (ML24064A039), partially approving publication and directing that Framework B be removed and treated separately.

The NRC published the proposed rule on October 31, 2024 at 89 FR 86918 under RIN 3150-AK31 and Docket NRC-2019-0062. The comment period was extended to February 28, 2025 by a notice published November 22, 2024 at 89 FR 92614. The final rule was published at 91 FR 15694 on March 30, 2026, with an effective date of April 29, 2026, approximately 21 months before the statutory deadline. The Commission's press release NR-26-035 describes Part 53 as the first new initial-licensing pathway since Part 52 in 1989 and the first major restructuring of reactor licensing standards since Part 50 in 1956.
bullets: NEIMA Section 103(a)(4) deadline: December 31, 2027
SECY-20-0032 (Apr. 13, 2020) and SRM-SECY-20-0032 (Oct. 2, 2020) initiated the rulemaking
SECY-23-0021 (Mar. 1, 2023) and SRM-SECY-23-0021 (Mar. 4, 2024) shaped the proposed rule
Proposed rule: 89 FR 86918 (Oct. 31, 2024); comment close extended to Feb. 28, 2025
Final rule: 91 FR 15694 (Mar. 30, 2026); effective Apr. 29, 2026

faqs.question: Where in Part 53 are the dose criteria for design-basis accidents?
faqs.answer: Section 53.210 in Subpart B. For each design-basis accident, the applicant must demonstrate that an individual at any point on the exclusion area boundary for any 2-hour period following onset of release, and an individual at the outer boundary of the low population zone during the entire passage of the radioactive cloud, would not receive a total effective dose equivalent in excess of 25 rem. Section 53.210 reiterates the longstanding position from 10 CFR 50.34 and 50.67 that the 25 rem value is a regulatory yardstick rather than an acceptable emergency dose limit. Compliance is shown using accepted methods and source terms.

faqs.question: Does Part 53 require a probabilistic risk assessment for every plant?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. Section 53.450(a) requires a PRA, other systematic risk evaluations (SREs), or a combination, deliberately permitting SREs in lieu of a full Capability Category PRA for designs where a full PRA would be disproportionate, such as very low source-term microreactors. Where a PRA is used, section 53.450(c) requires it to be maintained at least every 5 years until permanent cessation under section 53.1070, and upgraded per consensus standards endorsed by the NRC, including Regulatory Guide 1.247 for non-LWR PRA and Regulatory Guide 1.200 for LWR PRA, with codes qualified for their conditions of use.

faqs.question: How does Part 53 handle siting in higher population density areas?
faqs.answer: Section 53.530 in Subpart D permits siting in higher population density areas, including population centers exceeding roughly 25,000 residents, if the applicant demonstrates that societal risk is acceptable in light of societal benefits. This is a deliberate departure from the low-population-zone preference embedded in 10 CFR Part 100 for Part 50 and Part 52 plants. The applicant still must address geology, seismology, hydrology, meteorology, demographics, and emergency planning, and must demonstrate that site conditions do not undermine the Subpart B safety criteria. Detailed implementation guidance is being issued through a revised Regulatory Guide 4.7.

faqs.question: What is the seismic regime under Part 53?
faqs.answer: Part 53 generalizes the probabilistic approach in 10 CFR 100.23 and Regulatory Guide 1.208, anchoring the design earthquake to a site-specific Ground Motion Response Spectrum (GMRS) and pairing it with graded Seismic Design Categories drawn from ASCE/SEI 43-05 and ANSI/ANS-2.26. The categories tie SSC seismic design to performance goals defined by annual frequency of unacceptable performance and a limit state. The Safe Shutdown Earthquake and Operating Basis Earthquake pairing in 10 CFR Part 100 Appendix A and Appendix S to Part 50, including the 0.1 g floor, does not propagate into Part 53.

faqs.question: Can you load fuel into a reactor at the factory under Part 53?
faqs.answer: Yes, with conditions. Section 53.620(d) authorizes in-factory loading of unirradiated fuel into a reactor manufactured under a Part 53 manufacturing license before shipment, provided design features prevent the reactor from achieving criticality during transport and remain in place until NRC authorization is given at the installation site. The manufacturing license alone does not authorize operation. A separate Part 53 license is required to install and operate the manufactured reactor at its destination, with the installation-site application incorporating the manufacturing license by reference and confirming the as-shipped reactor falls within the approved envelope.

faqs.question: Who must hold an individual operator license under Part 53?
faqs.answer: It depends on the facility classification. For self-reliant-mitigation facilities, where the Commission determines the design does not require human action to prevent or mitigate accidents, sections 53.805 through 53.820 establish generally licensed reactor operators (GLROs) in lieu of individually licensed operators examined under Part 55. For interaction-dependent mitigation facilities, traditional licensed reactor operators and senior reactor operators continue to be required, examined under Part 55. Section 53.730 lets staffing levels be customized to the credited human actions in the safety analysis, and human factors expectations extend to remote operations centers.

faqs.question: Where does Part 53 address defense in depth?
faqs.answer: Section 53.250 in Subpart B. The provision requires measures for each plant to provide appropriate defense in depth to compensate for uncertainties in the safety analysis, so there is reasonable assurance that the Subpart B safety criteria are met over the life of the plant. The enumerated uncertainties include the state of knowledge and modeling capabilities, the ability of physical barriers to limit radionuclide release during non-DBA LBEs, the reliability and performance of plant SSCs and personnel, and the effectiveness of programmatic controls. Section 53.250 allows alternative measures rather than mechanically applying the single-failure criterion from Part 50 Appendix A.

faqs.question: What financial assurance methods are permitted for decommissioning?
faqs.answer: Section 53.1040 in Subpart G lists six categories: prepayment, external sinking fund, surety or insurance or guarantee, statement of intent for Federal licensees, contractual funding tied to non-bypassable customer obligations, and other mechanisms acceptable to NRC. The required amount is set under section 53.1020 from a site-specific decommissioning cost estimate and adjusted annually under section 53.1030 using either a site-specific factor or a generic factor of at least 0.65L + 0.13E + 0.22B drawing on Bureau of Labor Statistics regional data and NUREG-1307 waste burial charges. Section 53.1045 limits trust fund use to 3 percent for planning plus 20 percent after PSDAR.

faqs.question: Where is the FSAR content list in Part 53?
faqs.answer: The final rule places FSAR content in two parallel sections. Section 53.1369 governs operating license applications and section 53.1416 governs combined license applications. Earlier proposed-rule drafts numbered the COL FSAR section in the Section 53.140x range. Both sections require final site information, final design information equivalent to a standard design certification under section 53.1239, an integrity assessment program under section 53.870, emergency response facility descriptions, human factors analyses under section 53.730, training under section 53.830, emergency plans under section 53.855, QA under section 53.865, radiation protection under section 53.850, physical security under section 53.860 and Part 73, cybersecurity under section 73.54 or 73.110, fire protection under section 53.875, and proposed technical specifications under section 53.710(a).

faqs.question: What is the PSAR content list under Part 53?
faqs.answer: Section 53.1309 in Subpart H. The PSAR must describe the facility, its limits on operation, and a preliminary safety analysis at a level sufficient for the Commission to reach safety conclusions before issuing a construction permit. Required content includes site information equivalent to an early site permit under section 53.1146(a)(1)(iv) through (x); design information equivalent to a standard design certification per section 53.1239, including the QA program tied to Appendix B to Part 50; technical qualifications; preliminary emergency plans based on Appendix E to Part 50 or 10 CFR 50.160 for SMRs and non-LWRs; preliminary physical security under section 53.540; the Part 26 fitness-for-duty program; and a Safeguards Information program under 10 CFR 73.21 and 73.22.

faqs.question: How does Part 53 select licensing-basis events?
faqs.answer: Section 53.240 in Subpart B requires LBEs to be identified for each plant and analyzed under section 53.450 to demonstrate that the safety requirements are satisfied. LBEs must range from anticipated event sequences to very unlikely event sequences and must collectively address risk-informed combinations of SSC malfunctions, human errors, facility hazards, and external hazards. Section 53.240(c) requires the analysis to include one or more DBAs analyzed under section 53.450(f), confirm the adequacy of design features and programmatic controls needed to satisfy sections 53.210 and 53.220, and establish related functional requirements. Industry vocabulary (AOOs, DBEs, BDBEs, DBAs) comes from NEI 18-04 and Regulatory Guide 1.233.

faqs.question: When is the Part 53 final rule effective?
faqs.answer: The final rule was published at 91 FR 15694 on March 30, 2026, with an effective date of April 29, 2026, under RIN 3150-AK31 and Docket NRC-2019-0062. It arrived approximately 21 months before the December 31, 2027 statutory deadline imposed by Section 103(a)(4) of the Nuclear Energy Innovation and Modernization Act of 2018 (Pub. L. 115-439). The proposed rule was published earlier at 89 FR 86918 on October 31, 2024, with the comment period extended to February 28, 2025 by a notice published at 89 FR 92614 on November 22, 2024. Technical corrections to the final rule were republished April 13, 2026.

faqs.question: How does Part 53 govern security and fitness for duty?
faqs.answer: Part 53 itself does not house security requirements as stand-alone substantive provisions. Instead, security obligations are anchored in companion revisions to 10 CFR Part 73 issued alongside Part 53, providing a technology-inclusive, consequence-based approach across physical security, cybersecurity, and access authorization. Fitness for duty is implemented through a new Subpart M added to 10 CFR Part 26, which permits a broader range of biological specimens for drug testing and recognizes innovative screening technologies suited to small, modular, and remotely operated plants. Subpart F of Part 53 requires licensees to implement the security plans, cybersecurity plan, and access authorization program scaled to the design-basis threat.

faqs.question: What was the SECY trail that produced Part 53?
faqs.answer: Three Commission papers matter. SECY-20-0032, transmitted April 13, 2020, proposed the rulemaking plan and was approved via SRM-SECY-20-0032 on October 2, 2020 (ML20254A143). SECY-23-0021, transmitted March 1, 2023, contained the draft proposed rule organized around Framework A and Framework B; the Commission acted via SRM-SECY-23-0021 on March 4, 2024 (ML24064A039), partially approving publication, removing Framework B from the proposed rule, and directing a separate options paper. The proposed rule was then published October 31, 2024 at 89 FR 86918, and the final rule at 91 FR 15694 on March 30, 2026.

faqs.question: Does Part 53 replace 10 CFR Parts 50 and 52?
faqs.answer: No. Part 53 is an optional alternative. Subpart A states that satisfying Part 53 substitutes for the corresponding provisions of Parts 50, 52, 73, and others for applicants and licensees who voluntarily elect Part 53. Applicants may continue to license under Part 50 or Part 52 if they prefer. Part 53 was promulgated to give advanced reactors, including non-LWRs and novel LWR designs, a licensing pathway that does not require shoehorning into the deterministic LWR-centric structure of Parts 50 and 52. Per the NRC press release NR-26-035, it is the first new initial-licensing pathway since Part 52 in 1989.

NRC NEIMA Vision and Strategy for Advanced Reactors
10 CFR Part 50 Appendix S (earthquake engineering criteria)
